content.full_text:
FPPC
Jun 10 | 52FI'76

RICHARD CARPENTER
ATTORNEY AT LAW
1108 "H" STREET 32 Adler Circle
SACRAMENTO 95825

(916) 444-5700 488 - 5582

June 9, 1976

Gary Mondfrans
Vice Mayor
City of San Bruno
567 El Camino Real
San Bruno, Ca. 94066

A/l/6 - 5605

Dear Mr. Mondfrans:

A member of the city council who is also a member of the chamber of
commerce to which the council makes an appropriation for promotion
purposes is precluded from voting on the appropriation by the "remote
interest" provision of Section 1091 of the Government Code. That
section provides in part as follows:

"1091. Remote interest of officer or member

(a) An officer shall not be deemed to be interested in a
contract entered into by a body or board of which he is a
member within the meaning of this article if he has only a remote interest in the contract and if the fact of such interest
is disclosed to the body or board of which he is a member and
noted in its official records, and thereafter the body or board
authorizes, approves, or ratifies the contract in good faith
by a vote of its membership sufficient for the purpose without
counting the vote or votes of the officer or member with the
remote interest.

(b) As used in this article 'remote interest' means:

(1) That of a nonsalaried member or officer of a non-
profit corporation; ...

(c) The provisions of this section shall not be applicable to
any officer interested in a contract who influences or attempts
to influence another member of the body or board of which he is a
member to enter into the contract.

(d) The willful failure of an officer to disclose the fact of his
interest in a contract pursuant to this section shall be punishable
as provided in Section 1097. Such violation shall not void the contract however, unless the contracting party had knowledge of the fact
of the remote interest of the officer at the time the contract was
executed."

Vice Mayor Gary Mondfrans
Page Two
June 9, 1976

It was the addition of the word "member" by an amendment in 1973 sponsored
by the City of San Jose that extended application of the law to members.
Prior to the amendment, a council member would have had to be a nonsalaried
"officer" of the nonprofit corporation (chamber of commerce) to be required
to disclose and refrain from voting. I have suggested to the San Jose
representative in Sacramento that the law be restored to its pre-1973 status
but no legislation has been introduced to date to delete "member" from
Section 1091(b)(1).

In my opinion, there is nothing in Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act of
1974 which would prohibit a member of the council from voting on such an
appropriation. A council member who is an unsalaried officer or member of
the chamber of commerce has no personal financial interest in the appropriation
as would be required to prohibit his participation in the vote. Section 87100
provides:

"87100. Public officials; state and local; financial interest

No public official at any level of state or local government
shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use
his official position to influence a governmental decision in
which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest."

You also ask: "Is the local Chamber of Commerce member, in his many appearance
before a City Council, for and against various public issues, not limited to
issues solely affecting the Chamber of Commerce per se, is in that capacity
a lobbyist within the provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 or any
other act?"

The lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 apply only to
lobbying at the state level. See Sections 82039, 82026, 82066, 82039, 82037
and 82002 of the Government Code, all of which refer to "state" agencies or
the "state" Legislature. Only a policy of the chamber of commerce would limit
his council appearances to matters affecting the chamber and only a local
lobby registration and reporting ordinance of the City of San Bruno would
restrict his local lobbying activities. If you are interested in a local
lobbying ordinance you may wish to see Baron v. City of Los Angeles, 2 C.
3d 535 (1970), and the Los Angeles ordinance sustained by the California
Supreme Court in that decision. You should be able to get a copy of the
ordinance by writing to the League of California Cities or the City Clerk of
Los Angeles.

This letter should be discussed with your City Attorney whose opinion will be
controlling.
                                                    Sincerely,


RC:mvb                                              Richard Carpenter
cc: FPPC Attn: Jeanne Pritchard

City of San Bruno

567 EL CAMINO REAL, SAN BRUNO, CALIFORNIA 94066
(415) 583-3083

June 1, 1976

Mr. Richard Carpenter
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Carpenter:

At the League of California Cities conference held last month in Coronado
you stated that a Councilmember who is a member of the local Chamber of Commerce
could not vote on matters affecting the Chamber including the annual city
appropriation to the Chamber unless no other group existed which could provide
the same services as the Chamber provides.

I should very much like to receive your written confirmation on the above
statement.

I should also like information in regards to the following: is the local
Chamber of Commerce manager, in his many appearance before a City Council, for and
against various public issues, not limited to issues solely affecting the
Chamber of Commerce per se, is in that capacity a lobbyist within the provisions
of the Political Reform Act of 1974 or any other act?

Your prompt reply to these questions is appreciated as we are about to
enter Budget Sessions where these issues will be germane.

Sincerely yours,

Gary Nordfrans
Vice Mayor
